attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old woman is scheduled for caesarean delivery at 34 weeks gestation. She has had two previous caesarean sections. Antenatal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging demonstrate placenta percreta with invasion through the myometrium and into the bladder. Her haemoglobin is 115 g/L (115-160). She has no other medical history and takes only pregnancy vitamins.

The multidisciplinary team has decided to place pre-operative arterial balloons to reduce blood loss during the procedure. She has provided informed consent.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following arteries is the most appropriate site for balloon placement for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This case involves a high-risk caesarean delivery for placenta percreta, where significant blood loss is anticipated. Pre-operative arterial balloon placement is one strategy to reduce intraoperative blood loss in cases of placenta accreta spectrum disorders. Knowing the pelvic anatomy and uterine arterial blood supply is very relevant to make this decision.

Internal iliac arteries are the most appropriate site for balloon placement as they supply approximately 80% of uterine blood flow via the uterine arteries. Occlusion at this level provides effective reduction in uterine perfusion while maintaining collateral circulation.

Common iliac arteries would be too proximal and would unnecessarily compromise blood flow to the lower extremities. This could increase the risk of lower limb ischaemia without providing additional benefit in controlling uterine blood loss.

External iliac arteries supply the lower extremities and are not significant contributors to uterine blood flow. Occlusion at this site would not effectively reduce uterine perfusion.

Uterine arteries themselves would be technically more challenging to catheterise directly and would not provide any advantage over internal iliac artery occlusion. Additionally, there may be significant collateral circulation in cases of placenta percreta.

Ovarian arteries provide only a small proportion of uterine blood supply. Targeting these vessels alone would be insufficient to achieve meaningful reduction in intraoperative blood loss.

The placement of arterial balloons should be performed by experienced interventional radiologists as part of a planned, multidisciplinary approach to managing placenta accreta spectrum disorders. The balloons are typically placed in the early morning of surgery and removed post-operatively once haemostasis is achieved.
learningPoints: Internal iliac artery occlusion is the most effective site for balloon placement to reduce uterine blood supply in cases of placenta percreta
The uterus receives its primary blood supply from the uterine arteries, which are branches of the internal iliac arteries
Pre-operative arterial balloon placement can significantly reduce blood loss during caesarean delivery in cases of placenta accreta spectrum
references: Nankali A, Salari N, Kazeminia M, Mohammadi M, Rasoulinya S, Hosseinian-Far M. The effect prophylactic internal iliac artery balloon occlusion in patients with placenta previa or placental accreta spectrum: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Reprod Biol Endocrinol. 2021 Mar 4;19(1):40. doi: 10.1186/s12958-021-00722-3. PMID: 33663536; PMCID: PMC7931359.
